item.title: ‘Above all, there is love’: single mothers caring for disabled children – in pictures
item.description:
Carol Allen-Storey’s intimate images capture the struggles mums face looking after kids with disabilities – but also the huge hearts of both parents and children

Continue reading...
item.dc:creator: Guardian Staff

item.title: The Venezuela earthquakes – in pictures
item.description:
Two powerful earthquakes, a magnitude 7.5 and a magnitude 7.2, struck within minutes of each other causing buildings to collapse in the capital of Caracas, with high damage and casualties predicted

Continue reading...
item.dc:creator: Guardian Staff

item.title: World Cup 2026: Scotland gift victory to Brazil – in pictures
item.description:
Scotland faced the five-time world champions in their final group game in Miami, with qualification for the knockout stage in the balance

item.dc:creator: Jonny Weeks

item.title: The week around the world in 20 pictures
item.description:
Ukrainian strikes on a Moscow oil refinery, protests at the G7 summit, wildfires in Spain and Messi at the World Cup – the past seven days as captured by the world’s leading photojournalists

Warning: this gallery contains images some readers may find distressing

Continue reading...
item.dc:creator: Jim Powell

item.title: Giant cranes and a dragon boat festival: photos of the day – Friday
item.description:
The Guardian’s picture editors select photographs from around the world

item.dc:creator: Selected by Natasha Rees-Bloor

item.title: Fashion goes pop! How Yves Saint Laurent created photography magic – in pictures
item.description:
Yves Saint Laurent saw the power of photography to push boundaries and take risks that had an impact in the fashion world and beyond. The new exhibition Yves Saint Laurent and Photography, at New York’s International Center of Photography, includes nearly 300 iconic photographs and archival objects with images by artists including Richard Avedon, Annie Leibovitz, Irving Penn, Andy Warhol and others. Pairing photographs with contact sheets, campaign materials, magazines and personal images, the exhibit shows the vital role images played in legacy of the Yves Saint Laurent brand

Continue reading...

media:content.media:credit: Photograph: James Moore/© All rights reserved © Yves Saint Laurent
Photograph: James Moore/© All rights reserved © Yves Saint Laurent
Photograph: James Moore/© All rights reserved © Yves Saint Laurent

item.dc:creator: Curated by Briana Ellis-Gibbs

item.title: ‘It’s stronger than a drug!’ Transcendent portraits from Montreux jazz festival – in pictures
item.description:
From Quincy Jones to Sam Smith, Raye to Santana, Anoush Abrar has been taking mesmerising photographs of the festival’s stars for years

Continue reading...
item.dc:creator: Guardian Staff

item.title: Free as a bird: the Mexicans redefining gender – in pictures
item.description:
From gymnasts in kitten heels to lovers stalked by a devil, Pieter Henket’s dazzling portrait series, Birds of Mexico City, can feel like being in ‘a museum where the art comes alive’

Continue reading...
item.dc:creator: Guardian Staff

item.title: David Hockney – a life in pictures
item.description:
The artist from Bradford who rose to international fame with paintings such as A Bigger Splash has died aged 88. In later years he celebrated nature on vast canvasses

Continue reading...

item.title: ‘Pioneering photography’: early images of Newhaven’s fishers – in pictures
item.description:
When David Hill and Robert Adamson captured the lives of a small Scottish community in the 1840s, were they creating the first ever social documentary series? A fascinating new book makes the case

Continue reading...

media:content.media:credit: Photograph: Sam Drake/David Octavius Hill and Robert Adamson
Photograph: Sam Drake/David Octavius Hill and Robert Adamson
Photograph: Sam Drake/David Octavius Hill and Robert Adamson

item.dc:creator: Guardian Staff

item.title: The changing face of Mongolia as, beneath the grass, permafrost thaws
item.description:
The warming caused by climate breakdown in the landlocked east Asian country is transforming its fragile ecosystem

As the climate crisis accelerates, Mongolia is warming rapidly, transforming the country’s cryosphere, including some of the most southerly permafrost landscapes in the northern hemisphere.

Although rarely associated with the Arctic, Mongolia has a remarkably cold climate. Ulaanbaatar is the coldest capital city in the world, and a substantial portion of the country lies within the Arctic Ocean drainage basin. As a result, many of the physical and ecological processes occurring here resemble those found at much higher latitudes.

People in Khövsgöl province say they have observed an increase in the number of arrivals of migratory birds from China in recent years, consuming large quantities of fish in the region’s lakes. In northern Mongolia, communities closely tied to fishing, herding and tourism are witnessing the visible transformation of fragile freshwater ecosystems shaped by climate breakdown and the changing cryosphere.

Historical surveys conducted in the 1970s suggested that nearly 63% of Mongolia was underlain by permafrost. Today, estimates indicate that only 26% to 29% remains. Unlike the ice-rich permafrost of Siberia, Canada or Alaska, much of Mongolia’s permafrost is relatively warm, thin and dry, making it particularly sensitive to rising temperatures. Climate change is the primary reason for this decline, although local pressures such as overgrazing can further accelerate thaw by removing the vegetation that insulates the ground – Nikolay Shiklomanov, a professor in the department of geography and environment at George Washington University

Continue reading...
item.dc:creator: Natalya Saprunova

item.title: ‘It’s about the power of music and art’: Glyndebourne stages its first ever L’Orfeo – photo essay
item.description:
We go behind the scenes at rehearsals for William Kentridge’s new staging of Monteverdi’s 1607 opera l’Orfeo

‘L’Orfeo is an opera about opera. It’s an opera about the power of music. It’s about the power of art to construct the world,” says director and artist William Kentridge. Claudio Monteverdi’s 1607 work is, if not quite the first ever opera, the earliest opera still performed today, written when the form was in its very infancy.

Monteverdi called his work – composed for performance at the ducal court of Mantua, a “favola in musica – legend in music”. “Monteverdi was a genius,” says conductor Jonathan Cohen. “The piece is about the world’s most famous musician. He begins with a prologue where he has the allegorical character of La Musica [Music, here sung by Francesca Aspromonte, who also sings Eurydice in this production], who says ‘I am music, and I have the power to stop the birds singing, the power over nature.’ And of course Orfeo, the musician, has the power to control even the rocks, the trees, the animals and effect human emotions.”

William Kentridge (centre) during rehearsals, above, and, below, Kentridge talks with Francesca Aspromonte (La Musica/Euridice) and Roseline Wilkens (Euridice). Photograph by David Levene

Continue reading...
item.dc:creator: David Levene and Tristram Kenton, text by Imogen Tilden

item.title: Fields of the Gods: Mexico’s football pitches from above – photo essay
item.description:
In Mexico, football is played wherever space permits. The Reuters photographer Raquel Cunha spent three months taking photos of amateur matches across Mexico City and beyond

Across Mexico, a co-host of the 2026 World Cup, football pitches are laid out wherever communities can find the space. On the edges of towns, on highway underpasses, and even in a volcano crater, spaces are cleared that allow people young and old to share in the dream of the beautiful game.

In an impoverished neighbourhood in Monterrey, northern Mexico, 14-year-old Humberto Guadalupe, nicknamed “Messi” by friends and family, spends his weekends on the community’s only football field, surrounded by abandoned cars and dirt roads.

Humberto Guadalupe (left), 14, and Eduardo Reyes, 12, play football, followed by snacks organised by evangelists, in Monterrey

Continue reading...
item.dc:creator: Raquel Cunha; reporting by Cynthia Rodríguez

item.title: Kyiv picks up the pieces after another attack by Russia – photo essay
item.description:
On Monday night residents faced the third heavy assault on Ukraine’s capital in less than a month, as Putin appears to be trying to take advantage of a shortage of US-made air defence systems

In the northern residential suburb of Vynohradar – a district of modest apartment blocks – residents were quietly and calmly getting on with salvaging, clearing and dealing with what remained of their apartments after Monday night’s massive missile attack on Kyiv. Dozens of rockets and hundreds of drones had been let loose on the city, leaving five people dead.

A woman drinking coffee in her apartment, which was damaged in the night attack on the UNIT.City residential complex

Continue reading...
item.dc:creator: Charlotte Higgins in Kyiv. Photographs by Julia Kochetova

item.title: A Hopping good time: one of Europe’s largest travelling funfairs returns to Newcastle – in pictures
item.description:
The Hoppings funfair is believed to be one of the largest travelling funfairs in Europe, comprising hundreds of attractions set up on Newcastle upon Tyne’s Town Moor. The event, which originally began as a temperance fair in 1882, runs until 28 June and is expected to attract 500,000 visitors

Continue reading...
item.dc:creator: Oli Scarff / AFP / Getty Images

item.title: Burials and tears as Ebola outbreak continues to spread in DRC – in pictures
item.description:
Authorities in the Democratic Republic of the Congo say suspected Ebola cases have now passed 900 in the east of the country. The World Health Organization has said the outbreak poses a ‘very high’ risk

Continue reading...
item.dc:creator: Moses Sawasawa / AP

item.title: Take a trip on Route 66: still delivering kicks after 100 years
item.description:
The US’s most famous road celebrates its centenary. The 2,400-mile highway crosses eight states and three time zones from Chicago to LA

The Mother Road, as the author John Steinbeck called it, has evolved over the years from an escape for poor farmers fleeing the devastating dust storms of the 1930s to perhaps the quintessential American road trip route that’s still delivering kicks.

Although there have been faster and more direct routes between the nation’s second and third largest cities for some time, Route 66’s neon still burns brightly and its vintage signs beckon travellers to restored motor lodges, classic diners and roadside attractions.

Continue reading...
item.dc:creator: Susan Montoya Bryan/AP

item.title: Nepal’s uprising took their loved ones. Now they hope for change – gallery
item.description:
Seventy-seven people were killed during youth-led protests that toppled Nepal’s prime minister

Continue reading...
item.dc:creator: Photography by Navesh Chitrakar/Reuters
